title: Where Does the Money Go?
subtitle: Sort ChocoNation's money story before you build the full model.

gameplay.scenario: ChocoNation sells almond bars through kirana stores. A founder needs to see where each rupee belongs: money from customers, costs tied to each bar, monthly running costs, and items that are useful but do not belong in a simple profit-and-loss story. Sort these money items into the right bucket.

buckets.label: Direct Costs
buckets.hint: Costs that rise when you make or sell one more bar

buckets.label: Overheads (SG&A)
buckets.hint: Monthly costs to run the business

buckets.label: Not P&L
buckets.hint: Important cash or business items, but not this month's sales or costs

items.label: ₹30 received when one almond bar is sold
items.explain: This is revenue: money customers pay for the product. Revenue is the starting line of a profit-and-loss statement, not the profit itself.

items.label: ₹18 for cocoa, almonds, and the wrapper for one bar
items.explain: These are direct costs because they belong to each bar. If ChocoNation sells more bars, it needs more ingredients and wrappers.

items.label: ₹4 delivery charge for each bar sent to a kirana store
items.explain: This delivery cost moves with each sale, so it is a direct cost. It helps a founder calculate how much is left from each bar before overheads.

items.label: ₹12,000 monthly rent for the production kitchen
items.explain: Rent is an overhead: ChocoNation pays it even in a slow month. It is part of the running cost that sales must eventually cover.

items.label: ₹3,000 spent on a month of school-fair and social-media ads
items.explain: Advertising is an overhead because it supports the whole business, rather than one particular bar. Founders group running costs like this as SG&A.

items.label: ₹50,000 borrowed from a family member to buy a small mixer
items.explain: The cash is important, but borrowing is not revenue from customers. It changes the cash available and creates a repayment obligation, not this month's operating profit.

items.label: ₹20,000 paid once for a chocolate mixer
items.explain: A mixer helps the business for many months, so it is not treated like a single month's normal running cost in this simple P&L exercise.
